summary: Joel describes moving Joel on Software from a T1 line in the Fog Creek office to a colocation facility operated by Peer 1 Network. He explains why the T1 proved unreliable due to layers of outsourced providers (Savvis reselling Worldcom reselling Verizon), making outage resolution painfully slow. He walks through the colocation shopping process, comparing pricing tiers and bandwidth models, and explains why Peer 1 won out — they offered free hosting after seeing his site. The post concludes with a detailed account of physically setting up the Dell 2U server at the downtown Manhattan facility.
key_points: Outsourcing creates chains of responsibility that make problem resolution much harder — when Savvis had to convince Worldcom to convince Verizon to fix a line, it was 'like pushing on a string'
Static HTML generation (via CityDesk) handled 100K-500K daily hits on modest hardware, demonstrating that avoiding dynamic page generation eliminates most scaling concerns

The colocation market in 2003 split into three tiers: overbuilt dot-com-era companies heading for bankruptcy, scrappy small providers, and the rare company that combined quality with reasonable pricing
Colocation gives flexibility to run any software you want, unlike managed web hosting where you're constrained to the host's stack
Physical server setup details — dual power supplies, remote administration cards, packet filtering before going live — reveal the hands-on nature of early-2000s web infrastructure
money_quotes: Outsourcing is problematic that way: when everything works perfectly, you don't notice, but when things go wrong, it's much harder to get them fixed.
We had to convince Savvis to convince Worldcom to convince Verizon to fix it. Somehow this was like pushing on a string.
Since I publish the site using CityDesk which simply generates a bunch of plain HTML files, the server doesn't have to do anything tricky like generate each page from a database on the fly.

These were usually companies from the Dot Com days that built ridiculously fancy offices and have marble bathrooms in their colos and named gigantic sports stadiums after themselves, and are now in bankrupcy court.
If you ever get a choice of whether to deal with Canadians or New Yorkers, I know it's unpatriotic of me to say this, but, go with the Canadians.
Peer 1 is a small enough company that they care about your business, something which the giant bankrupt telcos don't.
We tried www.google.com, which redirected immediately to www.google.ca. Cool! They think we're in Canada!
key_insight: Layers of outsourced infrastructure create accountability gaps where no single provider owns the problem, making colocation with a responsive, right-sized provider more reliable than a theoretically superior but multi-layered ISP arrangement.
